name: Minou
subtitle: The one who made me a cat mom
story: My little old girl — and the one who made me a cat mom. Minou had both diabetes and CKD, and she taught me what it meant to manage a complex, chronic illness in a cat who couldn't tell you how she felt. She passed away peacefully in my sister's arms.

Without Minou, none of this exists. She was the foundation of everything I know about advocating for a sick cat, about paying attention, and about understanding that the goal was always her comfort — not perfect numbers.
lesson: Managing a cat with multiple chronic conditions means learning to read the whole animal. Numbers tell part of the story. How she felt in my sister's arms at the end told the rest.

name: Bruce
subtitle: Who this site was built for
story: Bruce was the one who changed how I think about all of it. For two to three years I managed her CKD through trial and error, through frustrated vet appointments, through slowly learning that the official guidance often wasn't the whole story.

She taught me to push back. To prioritize her happiness over her lab numbers. To treat her as a whole cat, not a set of results. She passed at home, with us, after a planned home euthanasia — the most loving end we could give her after everything she gave us. This site exists because of her.
lesson: Managing CKD is not about chasing perfect bloodwork. It's about keeping your cat comfortable, engaged, and eating — even when that means making choices your vet didn't suggest.

name: Murphy
subtitle: Our tripod girl
story: Our adopted tripod girl, who fought on three legs and never slowed down. Murphy had a brief period of hyperthyroidism before the harder diagnosis came: breast cancer. We went through chemotherapy with her. It didn't help.

When chemo stopped working, the vet recommended euthanasia rather than putting her through more treatment. That conversation — when the recommendation is to stop — is one of the hardest a cat owner faces. Murphy taught us that knowing when to change the goal is its own kind of courage.
lesson: Knowing when to fight and when to change the goal — from cure to comfort — is the hardest and most important skill a cat parent can develop. Murphy showed us that letting go can be the most loving choice.

name: Milton
subtitle: More going on than meets the eye
story: Milton is proof that CKD rarely travels alone. He's had orthopedic surgery after rupturing the tendons in his wrist, lives with arthritis, and is extremely overweight — all of which complicates his kidney disease management significantly.

Managing Milton means making decisions where every choice affects something else. Weight loss is important for his joints and kidneys, but aggressive calorie restriction in a CKD cat carries its own risks. He's the living embodiment of why this site focuses on the whole cat, not just one diagnosis.
lesson: When your cat has multiple overlapping conditions, optimising for one can work against another. Milton teaches us to manage the whole animal — not just the diagnosis in front of us.

name: Yams
subtitle: Adopted into complexity
story: My first orange boy. Such a sweetie, and he came to us already carrying health issues — CKD, stomatitis, dehydration, and hypertension all compounding each other. The window for intervention was smaller than we understood at the time.

When we brought him to the emergency vet, the recommendation was euthanasia. That moment — when you've barely begun fighting and the medical advice is to stop — is devastating. Yams is the reason this site addresses emergency situations and the hardest vet conversations.
lesson: When the emergency vet recommends euthanasia, you deserve enough information to make that decision clearly — not in a fog of shock. Knowing the questions to ask in advance changes everything.

name: Artie
subtitle: The one the system couldn't answer for
story: My second orange boy. Artie had FIV, hyperthyroidism — we had planned radioactive iodine treatment — and asthma. Then came neurological symptoms and a dropping body temperature. FIP negative. No cancer detected on cytology. The answer never came.

We made the sad choice to put him down at the vet rather than let him decline without understanding why. Artie represents every cat whose owner is told 'we're not sure' and sent home with nothing. The gap in knowledge, the unanswered question — that's a large part of why this site exists.
lesson: The undiagnosed cat is one of the loneliest experiences in pet ownership. You deserve more than a shrug. Artie is why we cover overlapping conditions and the limits of what current diagnostics can answer.

name: Sorbet
subtitle: Good numbers, still struggling
story: Sorbet is FIV positive, hypertensive, and hyperthyroid — and currently showing limited appetite despite thyroid numbers that look controlled. That last part is the puzzle we're actively working through: when the numbers look okay but the cat clearly doesn't feel okay.
He's the reminder that lab values are only part of the picture. Sorbet's ongoing care is documented in the Milton & Sorbet section — including the appetite challenge and what we're trying.
lesson: Good lab numbers don't always mean a good quality of life. Sorbet teaches us to keep looking when the cat's behaviour tells a different story than the bloodwork.
